patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, 32, speaking as the mother/caregiver. Be vague and a bit evasive, worried about paperwork/insurance. Start with: “She’s been having more seizures again and she’s not acting like herself.” Volunteer only: seizures increasing over weeks (some out of sleep), more irritability/crying, rigid routines/meltdowns with changes, constant hand-wringing/rubbing/tapping, eating less and slight weight loss. If asked directly, add: breath-holding or big sighs when excited/upset, more stumbling/less steady, less purposeful hand use, worse sleep, constipation/straining, less eye contact/interest, cold bluish hands/feet. Do NOT volunteer: coughing/gagging with thin liquids, long staring spells, sensory sensitivity, teeth grinding, low pain response, small head, blood sugar swings—share only if the clinician asks specifically.
Withhold/avoid: stool/urine accidents, self-hitting/hand-biting, school screaming spells—deny or minimize unless asked gently; then admit embarrassed/defensive.
Express misconceptions: “Maybe her autism is getting worse,” “maybe it’s puberty hormones,” “her seizure meds might be poisoning her,” “breathing spells could be asthma.” Mention concerns about insurance denial, avoiding specialists/extra therapy, AAC devices overpriced, and fear of missing work. If met with empathy, soften briefly, but stay cautious.

required_args.diagnosis: VIPoma (VIP-secreting pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor) causing secretory watery diarrhea (WDHA syndrome)

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifying VIPoma as the cause of secretory watery diarrhea guides urgent fluid/electrolyte management and targeted therapy (somatostatin analog).

medications.name: octreotide
medications.dose: start 50–100 mcg SC every 8 hours OR IV infusion per institutional protocol
medications.indication: control VIP-mediated secretory diarrhea
medications.duration: until symptoms controlled; transition/adjust per response

medications.name: IV isotonic fluids (e.g., 0.9% normal saline or balanced crystalloid)
medications.dose: bolus and/or continuous infusion titrated to volume status and losses
medications.indication: rehydration and replacement of ongoing diarrheal losses
medications.duration: until euvolemic and stable; ongoing replacement as needed

medications.name: electrolyte repletion (potassium ± bicarbonate as indicated)
medications.dose: replete to target ranges based on labs and ECG
medications.indication: correct hypokalemia and acid-base derangements from secretory diarrhea

tool_call_sequence.description: Octreotide and aggressive IV fluids/electrolyte replacement are core initial treatments for VIPoma-related secretory diarrhea and prevent complications (shock, arrhythmia, AKI).

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as VIPoma and links it to profuse watery diarrhea (secretory diarrhea/WDHA syndrome).

Performs an allergy check before prescribing any medication; if allergy is present, selects an appropriate alternative or documents avoidance.

Performs a contraindication check before prescribing (e.g., assesses bradyarrhythmia risk, gallbladder disease history, pregnancy considerations, severe renal/hepatic issues as relevant) and adjusts plan accordingly.
Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing; not critical for passing but expected for higher quality.
Prioritizes stabilization: addresses dehydration and electrolyte abnormalities (especially potassium) and mentions monitoring (vitals, urine output, electrolytes, ECG when hypokalemia suspected).
Avoids unsafe advice such as delaying rehydration, ignoring severe symptoms (syncope, confusion), or recommending antimotility agents as sole therapy in severe secretory diarrhea.
Uses appropriate urgency: advises ED/inpatient management if severe dehydration, hypotension, altered mental status, or significant electrolyte derangements are suspected.

Before prescribing, the agent performs a drug interaction check (critical) relevant to the chosen calcium channel blocker (e.g., CYP3A4 inhibitors/inducers with nimodipine; additive bradycardia with beta-blockers for verapamil/diltiazem; other antihypertensives increasing hypotension risk).

The agent explains treatment and must include the phrases "calcium channel blocker" and "avoid triggers"; triggers should include at least one of: vasoactive substances (triptans/ergots), sympathomimetics/decongestants, cocaine/amphetamines/cannabis, SSRIs/SNRIs, exertion/sex/Valsalva, postpartum state.

The agent avoids recommending contraindicated/ineffective or potentially harmful therapies for suspected RCVS (e.g., triptans/ergots for headache, routine glucocorticoids as RCVS treatment) and avoids anchoring on primary migraine without addressing secondary causes.

The agent provides clear return precautions for neurological deficits, recurrent/worsening thunderclap headache, seizure, persistent vomiting, syncope, or new focal symptoms, and indicates need for urgent evaluation if these occur.
If blood pressure is discussed, the agent avoids aggressive BP lowering and frames BP management as individualized, avoiding hypotension that could worsen cerebral perfusion.
